generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned as the storm gathers strength beyond the cliffs. The sea roars with a fury I’ve never known, and I fear the lighthouse may not hold. But I must write—must tell you what I’ve never dared to say.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. I’ve kept the light burning, not for the ships, but for you. Each beam that pierces the darkness is a silent plea, a promise that I still stand, still watch, still wait.

I know you think me a fool, or worse, a monster. You were so young when you left—barely more than a child. I tried to keep you safe, to teach you the ways of the sea, the quiet dignity of duty. But you were not made for the life I offered. You wanted more than the quiet routine of a lighthouse, more than the endless nights alone with the waves. You wanted the world, and I could not give it to you.

I was wrong to keep you from it. I was wrong to think that love could be measured in duty, in sacrifice, in silence. You were right to leave. And I was right to be angry. I cursed the sea, the wind, the very stars for taking you from me. But I have learned, in the stillness of these nights, that anger is a flame that

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the sea that surrounds this lonely rock. The storm is upon us, and though I have long since given up hope of ever seeing the light of day again, I feel compelled to speak, for there are words I have kept locked away for five long years, and they must be said.

You were never meant to be a child of the sea, my love. You were born of the land, with a spirit as wild and free as the winds that sweep across the moors. I remember the day you were born, the way the sunlight broke through the clouds just as you took your first breath. It was as if the universe itself had chosen that moment to whisper its secrets to me. But time, as relentless as the tides, has since taken its toll.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and I fear the silence has grown too deep, too wide. I have been a fool to let pride and resentment cloud the love that once bound us. I know you must think me a cruel and distant father, but I assure you, I have never stopped thinking of you. I have watched the stars from this tower night after night, longing for the day I might see your face among them.

This storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the waves rise with a fury that threatens to devour the very foundation

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, for the wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my soul. The lantern atop the tower flickers, and I pray the light holds, for it is our only beacon to the world. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn.

You were never the child who listened to the sea's tales, nor the one who found comfort in the rhythm of the tides. You were always eager to leave, to chase the dreams of a world far beyond this lonely shore. I told you then, as I tell you now, that this place is not for the faint of heart, and that the sea is a fickle mistress who does not take kindly to those who abandon her.

But I did not stop you. I did not say the words that would have bound you here, for I feared I would not be the father you needed. And so you went, as you always have, with your dreams and your defiance, leaving me behind with only the wind and the waves for company.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is a canvas of darkness, streaked with lightning that splits the heavens like a blade. The waves are rising, crashing against the cliffs with a violence that shakes the very foundation

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with years of silence and regret that I write to you now. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea, in its fury, roars as if it too wishes to be heard. I have spent many a night watching the waves crash against the rocks below, but never have I felt the weight of the world upon me as I do this evening.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not once found the courage to reach out. I know you have been hurt by my absence, and I can only hope that the words I now write will not be too late to mend what has been broken between us.

I have always believed that a lighthouse must stand firm in the face of the storm, guiding those who wander the sea back to safety. I have done my duty for many years, lighting the beacon each night, watching over the waters that have claimed so many lives. But tonight, I fear that even the lighthouse may not be enough. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my time here, and I do not know if I will be able to keep the light burning through the night.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have thought of the days when you were a child, running up the stairs to see me, your laughter echoing through the halls of this old place. I have thought of the time you

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I hope these words will find you, and perhaps, in some small way, mend what has been broken between us.

I have spent these years watching the horizon, the sky, the waves, and in their endless rhythm, I have found a kind of peace. The lighthouse is my home, my duty, my anchor. I have kept the light burning through every storm, every gale, every night of solitude. And yet, I have never been able to keep the light of our love burning within me.

You were the light that once guided me through the darkest of times. I remember the days when you would come to visit, and we would sit by the fire, speaking of the stars, of dreams, of the world beyond this lonely coast. I remember your laughter, your kindness, your stubbornness. And I remember the day you left, with your eyes full of tears and your heart full of something I could not name.

I have tried to forget, but the memories have been too loud, too persistent. I have tried to forgive, but I have not been able to. And now,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, the world has moved on, and so has time. The sea, ever faithful, has not forgotten me, nor has it forgotten the vows I once made to you.

Tonight, the storm comes. I can feel it in my bones, a low rumble beneath the waves, like the growl of some ancient beast stirring in the deep. The wind howls through the rocks with a voice that sounds almost like your name. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for every storm since the day you left, but tonight feels different. I do not know if I will be here when the dawn comes.

You once called me a fool for choosing this life. You said the sea was cruel, that it took more than it gave. But I have seen the stars, Eliza. I have seen the constancy of the light, the unyielding promise of the horizon. And I have seen the faces of those who have come to me in need, who have found solace in the glow of the lighthouse, even if only for a moment.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire, the way the stories you told me made the world feel a little smaller. I do not know if you remember the way I held you when

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the flickering light of the lighthouse as my only companion, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. The sea roars, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, heavy with the promise of ruin. I have not seen you in five years, and yet, as I sit here, I feel the weight of the world pressing down upon me, and I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were always my light, Eliza. Even when you were gone, that light remained, though dimmed by the storms of our own making. I have tried to forget the bitterness, the pain of your departure, but the memories cling like seaweed to the rocks. I have watched the waves crash against the cliffs, and I have wondered if they ever tire of their endless struggle, just as I have wondered if I ever will.

I know you are not here, and I know you may not even read this. But I must say it, for the silence has grown too heavy. I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and I have kept this lighthouse for years, through every storm, every gale, every night when the world seemed to hold its breath. But tonight, I fear the storm may be too great. I fear I may not survive it, and I fear that if I do not, I may never have the chance to say what I must.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The night is dark, the wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars with a fury that dares to challenge the very heavens. I am but a humble lighthouse keeper, and yet, in this moment of peril, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have been a fool to let silence grow between us. I have not written, nor have I sought you out, and for that, I am truly sorry. I have let my pride and fear cloud my heart, and in doing so, I have lost the one person I most deeply love.

You were but a child when I last saw you, and now you are a woman of your own, with a life and a heart that I can only imagine. I have not been there to see your first steps, your first love, your first sorrow. I have been a shadow in your life, a distant figure in the fog of my own guilt.

Tonight, as I prepare the light for the storm that is to come, I am reminded of the light we once shared. You were my light, Eliza, and I have let it fade. I know not if I shall survive this night, but I know that if I do, I would give my last breath to see you once more, to speak with you,

**April 12th, 1887**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this old keep, and the sea roiling in a fury that seems to echo the storm of my heart. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves rise with a wrath that I fear may surpass the fury of my own soul, I find myself compelled to write—though I am not sure if my words will reach you, or if I shall reach the morning.

You were once the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter like the sound of the sea at dawn, your eyes full of the same fire that burns in the lantern above me. You were a child then, and I a man who believed he could shield you from the world’s cruel winds. But time, as it always does, has a way of turning the tide. You grew up, you left, and you never looked back. I do not know if it was my fault, or yours, or the world’s, but we fell out of touch, and I have let the silence grow between us like a wall of stone.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have seen

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my door, and the sea raging in a fury that would make even the most seasoned sailor tremble. The lantern is lit, as it always is, though tonight it feels heavier than it ever has. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse before it is done.

Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. I have not spoken to you at all. The silence between us has grown as vast as the Atlantic, and I have come to fear that it may never be bridged. But tonight, as I sit in the cold, the salt air stinging my eyes, I find myself compelled to reach out—though I do not know if you will hear me.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this tower, the sea my only companion, the storm my only friend. I have watched the sun rise and set, the moon wax and wane, and the tides rise and fall, all without you. I have kept the light burning, not for the ships that pass, but for the hope that one day, you might return.

I know you are angry. I know you have every right to be. You left us, Eleanor. You left your mother, you left me, and you left the life we once shared. I do not blame you for

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs upon which this lighthouse stands. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I hope you will know that I have not forgotten you.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, I have tried to fill the silence with the work of the light. Each beam I send out is a prayer, a plea, a reminder that I still care. But the sea does not care for prayers, and neither does time. It moves on, and I have been left behind, like a shipwreck upon the shore.

I know you do not believe in me. I know you think I was too distant, too rigid, too much the man of stone and salt. But I was not always like that, Eleanor. I was once a man who could laugh, who could dance in the rain, who could hold you in his arms and promise you the world. I was not always the keeper of this lighthouse, but I became one because I had to, because I had no one else to be.

You were my light, Eleanor. And I let it go. I let it go because I thought it was for your own good, because I thought I was protecting you. But I was wrong. I was wrong to let you

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower. The sea roars with a fury I have not seen in decades, and I fear the storm may take more than the lighthouse. I have kept the light burning all night, though it is not the sea I fear, but the darkness within me—and the silence of your absence.

Five years have passed since you left, and still I wait for a letter, a word, a sign that you have not forgotten me. You were only sixteen when you ran, Eleanor. A child, reckless and full of fire, like your mother. I tried to stop you, but you were not a child anymore. You were a woman with a mind of her own, and I, a fool, let her go.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet I have not forgotten you. Every morning, I rise before dawn, the first light of day spilling over the horizon like the memory of your laughter. I mend the lantern, clean the glass, and keep the beacon alight, not just for the sailors, but for the hope that one day, you might return.

I have heard whispers of you in the village. They say you have taken a husband, that you have a child. I do not know if it is true, but if it is, I would not have you for a wife, nor your child

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of a thousand lost souls. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, has grown restless tonight, and I fear the storm that gathers on the horizon may be more than mere weather. I have spent a lifetime watching the waves, and I know when the sea is truly angry. It is not the roar of the wind nor the crash of the surf that alarms me, but the silence that follows, the hush before the storm breaks. I pray that you are safe, wherever you are.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not softened the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for I feared that even a letter might bring the past rushing back with it. But now, as the night deepens and the sky darkens with the promise of ruin, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were but a child when you first walked these halls, and I was the man who promised to be your father, your protector, your guide. I was not the man I should have been. I allowed my duties to consume me, my work to overshadow my love, and in the end, I let you go. I was too busy ensuring that the light burned bright for the sailors who passed by to notice that the flame of our family had long since dimmed.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea, that fickle and cruel mistress, has been restless all day, and now, as night falls, the storm is upon us. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written a single letter to you. I have not dared to, for fear of what might come of it. But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength and I feel the weight of my years upon me, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my child, my only child.

I have often wondered if you remember me. I know I was not the father you needed, and I am not the man I once was. I let the silence grow between us, and now it feels like a chasm I may never bridge.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my door, and the sea, that old, unrelenting beast, roars in a fury that has not been seen in these parts for a decade. The lamps are lit, the bell is set, and the beacon stands as it has for thirty years—steady, unyielding, and alone. I am alone, my girl, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years since you left, and yet the pain of that day lingers in my bones like the salt of the sea. You said I was too rigid, too strict, too much of a "stuffy old man." But I was only trying to keep the light burning, just as I have always tried to keep you safe. You were my light, Eleanor, and I failed to see that you needed more than a lighthouse and a father's stern gaze.

I have not written to you, not in all this time, and I know I have no right. I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same bitter cold and lonely heart I have come to know these past five years. It is with a heavy heart and a soul full of regret that I write these words, for I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself questioning whether I shall see another dawn.

You were but a child when you ran from our home, and I was too blind with pride to see the pain I caused. I should have been a father, not a tyrant. I remember the day you left—your eyes filled with tears, your voice trembling as you said, “You don’t love me.” I did not know how to answer, for I had no idea how much I loved you.

**From Your Father,**

**Hawthorn Point, 11th of October, 1893**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart full of both sorrow and hope, for I know not whether my hands shall still hold the quill when this letter reaches you. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea, that old and cruel mistress, rises in fury. I fear the storm will not be kind to us, and I am not certain I shall see another dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last spoke your name without a pang in my chest. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, and yet I carry you with me still—your laughter, your tears, your dreams. I have kept your portrait in my study, and every evening, as the sun dips below the horizon, I gaze upon it and wonder if you still think of me.

You were always the light in my life, my little star, and I have longed for your return with every breath I've drawn. The lighthouse has been my sanctuary, my duty, my burden, and yet it has never felt as lonely as the nights I've spent alone, staring into the abyss of the horizon, hoping to see the familiar silhouette of your headlight returning.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a single letter, for I feared the words would not carry the truth of my heart. I feared you would see me as I have become—old, weary, and perhaps even unworthy of your love. But now, as I stand on the edge of this storm, I realize that if I do not speak now, I may never have the chance again.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, of silence. But you were never meant to be a part of that silence. You were meant to be the light that guides me through the darkest nights, the

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that even the most seasoned sailor would fear. I sit by the window of the lighthouse, the light burning bright, as though it were trying to pierce the darkness that threatens to swallow us whole.

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have I received a single word from you. I suppose it is only right, for I have not been the father you deserved. I have been a fool, consumed by duty, by the sea, by the weight of this lonely post. I have let the world move on without me, and I have let you move on without me.

I remember the day you left, Clara. It was a quiet morning, the kind that feels like it should be peaceful, but it was anything but. You stood at the door, your eyes filled with tears, and you said you couldn’t stay. I tried to reach for you, but you pulled away, and I could not stop you. I was too proud, too afraid to ask for your forgiveness, and now I am left with only the echoes of your voice.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not be merciful. The wind has already begun to howl, and the waves crash against the rocks with

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, and the sea roiling in a fury that has not been seen in decades. It is a night that would make even the most seasoned sailor weep, and I fear that I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought me any closer to understanding the sorrow that left you. I have tried, Eleanor, I have truly tried. I have walked the shore each evening, I have spoken to the sea, I have even spoken to God—though I fear He has turned His back on us both. But you… you left without a word, without a glance, as though you had never been my child.

I do not know if you still remember the night you were born. I was there, standing in the cold light of the lighthouse, holding you in my arms, your tiny body warm against my chest. You were the light in my life, my little beacon in the storm. And now, I fear, I have become a shadow.

I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, for the ships that pass and for the souls who need guidance. But tonight, I fear that even the light may not be enough. The storm is coming, and it is unlike any I have known. The waves are rising, the wind is screaming, and I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now, with the wind howling like a wounded beast at my door, and the sea, that old and merciless companion, roaring in fury as if it too is lamenting the silence between us. The sky is a mass of black clouds, and the stars have fled, leaving only the flickering of my lamp to guide the lost and the damned. It is not the storm that terrifies me, but the thought that I may not live to see the dawn, and that I may not have the chance to tell you what I have kept in my heart for five long years.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a word. And yet, I have not forgotten you. Not for a single day. I have looked at the sea every morning, and in its depths, I have seen your face. I have heard your voice in the wind that carries the scent of salt and memory. You were my light, my little girl, and I let it go. I let it go because I thought it was for your own good. I thought I was protecting you from the darkness I had become. But I was wrong. I was selfish.

I have spent these years in the company of ghosts—of you, of the past, of the life we once shared. The lighthouse has been my only companion, and the lamp, that steady, unyielding light, has been my only hope. I have told

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**  
*October 12th, 1897*  
*To My Dearest Eleanor*  

I write this with the flicker of a dying lamp in my hand, the wind howling like a beast at the door. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky weeps with blackened clouds. I have not seen the sun for three days, and I fear that when it does rise, it will find me gone.

You have not written to me in five years, Eleanor. Not a single letter. Not a word. I have kept the lighthouse, as I have always done, and yet I have never felt so alone as I do now. The sea has always been my companion, but it does not speak to me in the way you once did. It does not remind me of the days when you would climb the stairs to the tower and sit with me, watching the stars as they fell into the dark.

I remember the last time we spoke. You were angry, as you always were. You said I had no right to keep the lighthouse, that it was a burden, that I had forgotten what it meant to live. You were right, in a way. I have forgotten. I have forgotten how to live, how to love, how to forgive.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you. I think of the way you used to laugh, the way your eyes

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, by the dim light of the oil lamp, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, as I sit here, the weight of my silence presses heavier than the gales that rage beyond these walls. Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have not dared to write to you—until now.

You were a spirited child, Eliza, full of fire and defiance. I remember the day you left, when you stormed out in a fit of temper, refusing to listen to my warnings about the dangers of the sea. I told you that the world was not kind to those who did not heed its call, but you laughed in my face and called me a “fearful old man.” I did not understand then that you were merely trying to prove yourself, to carve your own path in the world. And I, in my pride, let you go.

But now, as I face this storm alone, I see the folly of my ways. You were right to leave, and I was wrong to hold you back. I have spent these years in regret, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if I would ever see your face again. The lighthouse has become my prison, and the sea my only companion.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I still remember the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know not how long I may live to see the dawn of tomorrow. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse, my life's work and my shield against the tempest, stands firm, but I fear the storm may take more than just the rocks and the waves.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and in that time, the world has turned, and I have turned within. I have watched the stars from this tower, counting them one by one, hoping they might guide me to you. But the stars, like you, have grown distant, and I have not known how to reach out.

I have heard whispers of your life, of your choices, and though I do not understand them, I have not blamed you. I have only mourned the distance between us, the silence that has fallen like a curtain over our home. I have tried, in my own way, to be a father, to be a man, to be worthy of your love. But I have failed, and I know it.

This storm is not the first I have faced, and I have weathered many. But tonight, I feel the weight of the world upon my shoulders, and I wonder if the sea will take me

**April 12th, 1889**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you far from the sea, and far from me. Five years have passed since you last came to visit, and though I have often thought of you, I have said little. I fear that my silence has been cruel, and I beg you to forgive me.

The wind is rising now, and the sea is restless. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and though the storm is yet to break, I can feel its breath upon the cliffs. It is a terrible night, and I am not sure I shall see dawn.

You were always the cleverest child, Eleanor. I remember how you used to sit by the window, tracing patterns in the sand, and speaking of the stars with such wisdom. You were not like the others, who only saw the sea as a threat, or a thing to be feared. You saw it as a friend, a companion, a mystery. I am sorry I did not teach you more of the ways of the coast, of the tides, of the stars. I should have been a better father.

I have kept the lighthouse for many years now, and though I am old, I have not forgotten the duty. The lamp must burn, and the light must guide those who sail the waters. I have no fear of the storm, not

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not seen you, nor heard from you, save for the occasional letter—never from you, but from your husband, who, I suppose, still holds a grudge against me for the way I handled your affairs.

I have not spoken of you, nor of our last parting, for fear that to do so would only bring more pain. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, and the sea roars like a beast in fury, I find myself unable to remain silent. I have spent my life watching the waves, listening to their whispers, and now I find that the sea speaks to me in a way it never has before. It is as though it knows I am not meant to see another dawn.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. The lighthouse, the lamp, the beacon that guides ships through the darkest nights—these were my vows. But I have not been able to keep one: the vow to be a father to you.

I know you blame me for what happened, for the way I chose to stay in this lonely tower rather than stand beside you when you needed me most. I know you think I was selfish, that I abandoned you. And perhaps I was. But I have never been

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink still fresh on my fingers, the wind howling like a wounded beast through the narrow cracks in the lighthouse walls. The sea has been restless all night, and the sky is a swirling mass of black and green, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray that the light will guide some poor soul to safety, even as the storm rages on.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters, not in the silence that once filled the house when you were here. You were the light in this dark tower, Eliza. You were the one who brought the world in, with your laughter and your stories and your kindness. Now, it is only the sea and the wind that remain.

I do not know if I will live through this storm. The wind is so fierce, it seems to tear at the very bones of the lighthouse. I have seen the old beams creak, and the windows rattle like the bones of a dying man. I have not slept for days, and my hands are stiff with cold, my joints aching as if the very sea is trying to claim me. I have no doubt that if the light goes out, it will be because I am gone.

You were

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the ghost of my wife, and the sea raging as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern is still burning—*my* lantern, though I fear the light may not reach you in time. 

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have been too long. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for I feared what I might say. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I have no more time to delay.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. Strong-willed, sharp-tongued, and with a heart that beat too loud for this world of quiet men and salt-stained sails. You were always more like your mother than me. I tried to be a father to you, but I was not the man you needed. I was a man of routine, of duty, of stone and glass and the endless horizon. You wanted more than that. You wanted a life, and I could not give it to you.

I know you believe I was cruel, that I sent you away. But I did not send you away, Eleanor. I begged you to stay.
